phrases.cultural_note: The Place de la Palud has a unique animated clock — every hour, mechanical figures re-enact a local legend about a witch and a shepherd.

responses.english: It's a mechanical clock. The figurines move every hour.
responses.phonetic: say oon or-LOZH may-kah-NEEK. lay fee-goo-REEN boozh toot lay zuhr
responses.french: C'est une horloge mécanique. Les figurines bougent toutes les heures.

phrases.cultural_note: The square hosts a market every Wednesday and Saturday morning. Local farmers sell produce, cheese, and crafts.

responses.phonetic: mehr-kruh-DEE ay sahm-DEE mah-TAN, duh weet uhr ah trez uhr
responses.french: Mercredi et samedi matin, de huit heures à treize heures.

phrases.text_local: Où se trouve l'Hôtel de Ville ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo seh TROOV loh-TEL duh VEEL
phrases.cultural_note: Lausanne's Town Hall (Hôtel de Ville) dates from 1666 and sits at the east end of the square. Its facade overlooks the market.

responses.english: Lausanne's Town Hall is located at the east end of Place de la Palud. It dates back to 1666.
responses.french: L'Hôtel de Ville de Lausanne se trouve à l'extrémité est de la Place de la Palud. Il date de 1666.

phrases.cultural_note: The Place de la Palud is pedestrianized and surrounded by cafés — a perfect spot for coffee, people-watching, and observing local life.

responses.english: Yes, it's pedestrian. There are cafes all around.
responses.phonetic: wee, say pyay-TOHN. eel ee ah day kah-FAY too oh-TOOR
responses.french: Oui, c'est piéton. Il y a des cafés tout autour.
